summary: Four psalms of lament and longing: David's morning prayer for protection from the wicked (Ps 5), his penitential cry under physical and relational suffering (Ps 38), his lament over betrayal by a close friend (Ps 41, quoted by Jesus of Judas in John 13), and the Sons of Korah's iconic psalm of exile and soul-thirst for the living God (Ps 42).
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
5Individual Lament / Morning PrayerDavidSurrounded by enemies and the arrogant; seeking God's guidance and protectionGod's hatred of evil contrasted with His steadfast love for those who take refuge in Him; the righteous access God's house by grace alonev.7 "But I, through the abundance of your steadfast love, will enter your house; I will bow down toward your holy temple in the fear of you."v.9 quoted in Romans 3:13 ("Their throat is an open grave") as evidence of universal human sinfulness
38Penitential Psalm / Individual LamentDavidPhysical illness David interprets as divine chastisement; abandoned by friends, attacked by enemies who repay evil for goodSuffering as consequence of sin; the isolation of the afflicted; honest confession before God as the only path forwardv.18 "I confess my iniquity; I am sorry for my sin."Anticipates Christ who bore the chastisement that brings our peace (Isaiah 53:5); the pattern of suffering-confession-restoration points to Gospel grace
41Individual Lament / TrustDavidSerious illness combined with betrayal by a trusted companion; enemies gloat and friends abandon himGod's blessing on those who consider the weak; the anguish of betrayal by a close friend; confidence that the LORD sustainsv.9 "Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me."Jesus quotes v.9 in John 13:18, applying it directly to Judas Iscariot's betrayal at the Last Supper
42Individual Lament / Song of LongingSons of KorahExile from Jerusalem and the temple; taunted by enemies; spiritual depression and longing for the presence of GodSoul-thirst for the living God; preaching to one's own downcast soul; hope maintained through self-exhortation even in the depths of despairv.1–2 "As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God. My soul thirsts for God, for the living God."Christ as the fulfillment of the soul's thirst (John 7:37–38); the recurring refrain ("Why are you cast down?") reappears in Psalm 43:5, forming a paired lament-hope unit
